Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have a solid study routine. I'd tell myself I'd study after my extracurricular activities, but then I'd just end up listening to podcasts or audio lectures while doing other stuff. Maybe I'd put in a few hours here and there, like 16 hours a week sounds about right, but it was scattered. I learn better by listening anyway, so I figured I could absorb things passively, but that didn't always work out for actually completing assignments.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd have good intentions at the start of each week, but then something would come up with my activities or I'd just lose track of time. Deadlines would hit and I'd scramble, but even then I wasn't always completing things. I guess I relied too much on thinking I'd catch up later, and later never really came in the way I planned.
Q3: Usually it was because I got a notification about something due soon, or I remembered I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in just to see what I was missing, which honestly made my anxiety spike. Other times my classmates would mention something in the discussion forums and I'd feel like I should at least see what was happening. It wasn't really a proactive thing for me.
Q4: Not really, if I'm being honest. There were maybe a couple weeks early on where I tried to make it a habit, like checking in every morning. But it never stuck. The LMS just felt like another thing demanding my attention, and with everything else going on, it slipped down my priority list. I never got to that autopilot stage with it.
Q5: The audio lectures and any recorded discussions were gold for me. I could download them or just play them while I was commuting or doing chores. That fit my learning style way better than reading dense PDFs. The discussion posts were also useful because hearing different perspectives helped things click for me, even if I didn't always contribute as much as I should have.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and the longer video content that required me to sit and watch actively. I just couldn't focus on those, especially when my anxiety was acting up. The supplementary resources and optional readings? Never touched them. I told myself I'd get to them but I knew deep down I wouldn't. They felt like extras I couldn't afford time-wise.
Q7: If something was quick and directly connected to what I needed for an assignment or exam, it felt worth it. Also, if it was audio-based or something I could engage with without staring at a screen, I was more likely to do it. Anything that felt like busywork or wasn't clearly going to help my grade? I just couldn't justify spending my limited energy on it.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff felt completely skippable to me. Required activities had this weight to them, even if I still didn't always complete them. Optional things were like suggestions I could ignore without immediate consequences. The problem is I ignored too much of the required stuff too, but at least with required things I felt some guilt about not doing them.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download audio files and listen to them on my phone when I wasn't near my laptop. I'd also talk through concepts with friends from my extracurriculars who were in similar courses. A lot of my studying happened through conversations and listening rather than anything trackable. I don't use edutech apps, so everything was pretty low-tech on my end.
Q10: There were times I'd be walking around campus with my earbuds in, replaying lecture recordings and really processing the material. Or I'd be in a group chat with classmates discussing problems verbally. None of that showed up anywhere. My actual understanding sometimes grew in those moments more than when I was logged into the system clicking through modules.
Q11: All the time. I'd download the audio lectures in one session and then not log back in for days while still listening to them repeatedly. Same with any PDFs I actually bothered to save—I'd have them on my phone and reference them offline. The LMS probably thought I disappeared, but I was still engaging with the content in my own way.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. My anxiety was really high even though I tried to stay resilient about it. I had a lot going on with my extracurriculars, and the assignments started piling up. I felt overwhelmed but also weirdly detached, like I knew I should care more but couldn't make myself act on it. That's when my assignment completion really tanked.
Q13: Absolutely. When I was tired or stressed from other responsibilities, the LMS was the first thing I dropped. I'd tell myself I'd catch up on the weekend, but weekends were for my other activities. The confusion about some assignment requirements also made me avoid them entirely rather than asking for help. It was easier to not engage than to feel stupid.
Q14: Yeah, sometimes I'd log in and just scroll through things without really absorbing anything. I'd watch a video but my mind would be elsewhere, thinking about other stuff or just feeling anxious. I'd technically be on the platform but I wasn't learning. It was more like going through the motions to feel like I was doing something.
Q15: All the time. I'd see an assignment and know it was important, but the anxiety would kick in and I'd convince myself I needed to be in the right headspace first. That right headspace rarely came. I'd do the mindfulness exercises they recommended, which helped my mood a bit, but it didn't translate into actually completing the work. I kept drifting away from what mattered.
Q16: Deadlines were the only thing that really got me moving, but even then, not enough. I'd log in more frequently as deadlines approached, maybe participate in a discussion or two. But I'd often miss the actual submission anyway because I'd underestimate how much time I needed. The deadline pressure helped my engagement spike temporarily but it wasn't sustainable.
Q17: When I actually got feedback, it sometimes motivated me briefly. Like if a professor commented on a discussion post I made, I'd feel seen and want to contribute more. But the feedback was often delayed or I'd avoid looking at it because I was scared it would confirm I was failing. So it was a mixed bag—helpful in theory, avoided in practice.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid, which didn't work great for me. I needed more flexibility and audio-based options. The weekly modules were fine in theory, but if I fell behind one week, it felt impossible to catch up. The structure assumed consistent engagement, and I just couldn't deliver that. It made me feel like I was always playing catch-up.
Q19: The lack of audio alternatives for a lot of content made it harder for me. I learn by listening, so reading-heavy modules were a struggle. Also, the assignment instructions were sometimes confusing, and I didn't always know where to ask for clarification without feeling embarrassed. When things were straightforward and had audio options, I engaged way more easily.
Q20: I think that's me in some ways. I'd log in, browse around, watch some stuff, participate in discussions a bit—but then not actually submit the assignments. It's like the final step of completing work felt like this huge hurdle. The anxiety around actually turning something in and being judged was paralyzing. Activity doesn't equal output, I learned that the hard way.
Q21: I can see how that happens. If someone's studying offline or has a really efficient approach, they might not need to be on the LMS constantly. For me, I had the offline studying part but not the performing well part. My exam scores were okay-ish but my assignments dragged me down. So low activity can mean different things depending on the student.
Q22: Logs can show when you're present and clicking around, but they can't show if you're actually thinking or learning. They miss all the offline stuff—the conversations, the listening while walking, the processing that happens away from the screen. They also can't capture anxiety or why someone might be avoiding certain things. It's just surface-level data.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about the material and wanting to understand it, not just going through the motions. It's about being mentally present, asking questions even if just in your head, and connecting what you're learning to something meaningful. I wanted to be engaged, I really did, but I kept drifting. Engagement is more than just showing up.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I actually thought about the course content during random moments, or how my anxiety made simple tasks feel impossible. It wouldn't show the conversations I had with friends about course topics, or how I'd replay lectures while trying to fall asleep. It also wouldn't show that I cared more than my completion rates suggest—I just couldn't translate that into action.
